sections.heading: Hill Climbing
sections.body: Hill climbing starts from a single current state and repeatedly moves to a better neighboring state, stopping when no neighbor is better. It's simple and memory-efficient (it doesn't track a whole search tree) but can get stuck at a local maximum — a state better than all its neighbors, but not the best possible state overall.

sections.heading: Simulated Annealing
sections.body: Simulated annealing addresses hill climbing's local-maximum problem by sometimes accepting a worse move, with a probability that decreases over time (following a "temperature" schedule inspired by metal cooling and annealing). Early on it explores more freely; later it settles down, converging toward a strong solution.

sections.heading: Local Beam Search
sections.body: Local beam search keeps track of k states in parallel (instead of just one, as in hill climbing) and generates successors for all of them, keeping the best k overall at each step — the parallel states can share information, since a promising region discovered by one "pulls" the search toward it.

sections.heading: Genetic Algorithms (Overview)
sections.body: Genetic algorithms maintain a population of candidate solutions and evolve it over generations using operations inspired by biological evolution: selection (favoring fitter candidates), crossover (combining parts of two candidates), and mutation (small random changes) — searching for good solutions without needing gradient or heuristic guidance in the traditional sense.

quiz.question: The main weakness of basic hill climbing is:
options: It can get stuck at a local maximum, missing the global best solution
It always finds the optimal answer

quiz.question: How does simulated annealing address hill climbing's weakness?
options: By sometimes accepting worse moves, with decreasing probability over time, to escape local maxima
By tracking the entire search tree

quiz.question: Genetic algorithms use which biologically-inspired operations?
